en.extract:
The blue-and-white flycatcher (Cyanoptila cyanomelana) is a migratory songbird in the Old World flycatcher family Muscicapidae. The species is also known as the Japanese flycatcher. It breeds in Japan, Korea, and in parts of north eastern China and the Russian Far East. It winters in South East Asia, especially in Vietnam, Cambodia, Thailand, Sumatra and Borneo. This species has been recorded as a vagrant from the Sinharaja Rainforest in Sri Lanka in 2014.


Taxonomy and etymology
The species was first described in 1829 by Coenraad Jacob Temminck under the binomial name Muscicapa cyanomelana, and was transferred to its own genus Cyanoptila by Edward Blyth in 1847, on the basis of structural differences from other flycatchers, including its wing length and bill dimensions.
The genus name comes from ancient Greek; "kuanos" meaning dark blue, and "ptilon", plumage. The specific cyanomelana also comes from "kuanos", paired with melas, melanos meaning dark, black.


Subspecies
Two subspecies are accepted:

C. c. cyanomelana (Temminck, 1829) - breeds in Japan and the southern Kuril Islands; it migrates south to winter in Myanmar, Thailand, and the Greater Sundas.
C. c. intermedia (Weigold, 1922) - breeds in northeastern China (Heilongjiang southward to northeastern Hebei, north and east of Beijing), southeastern Russia (Amur Oblast and Primorsky Krai), and the Korean peninsula; it migrates south to winter in Myanmar, Thailand, and the Greater Sundas.
A former third subspecies from further southwest in China is now treated as a separate species, Zappey's flycatcher C. cumatilis.


Description
The blue-and-white flycatcher is a large flycatcher (16–17 cm long, weight 25 g on average) with a striking plumage pattern. The male is deep cobalt blue, with black on the face down to the upper part of the breast and flanks, the rest of the underside white. There is also a prominent white patch on the base of the outer tail feathers. Immature males have the adult male's blue wings, but are otherwise brown like females.
The female is smaller on average than the male, with head, face and most of upperparts grey-brown, and pale beige-white on the lower part of the middle of the throat, young males are very similar. The female has a smaller-headed profile than other brown flycatchers in range.
The song is a short, melancholic sounding series of whistles.


Ecology


Habitat
Blue-and-white flycatchers breed in primary and secondary lowland and submontane forests (including taiga, wooded slopes, and gullies) up to 1200 metres, and also in riverine thickets and plantations. Outside the breeding season, it uses coastal woodland, scrub, parks, and gardens, and in Borneo winters at elevations up to 1850 metres in forested hills, lower montane forest, plantations, and logged lowland forest.
These flycatchers are most active in the morning.


Breeding
The breeding season extends from late May to early August. The nests, constructed primarily of moss, are typically situated in a variety of sheltered locations, including crevices in cliffs, among tree roots, under overhanging stream banks, in shallow tree hollows, and, rarely, within abandoned buildings. The clutch size ranges from four to six eggs, which are incubated solely by the female for a period of 14 to 15 days. Females spend more time and energy caring for young than males. Nest parasites include the northern hawk-cuckoo, common cuckoo and lesser cuckoo.


Diet
It feeds mainly on insects and larvae, but also eats berries, including pokeweed.


Status
Although the species population is decreasing, its wide distribution and likely large population size lead to its classification as "Least Concern" on the IUCN Red List.


Gallery


References

Del Hoyo, J.; Elliot, A. & Christie D. (editors). (2006). Handbook of the Birds of the World. Volume 11: Old World Flycatchers to Old World Warblers. Lynx Edicions. ISBN 84-96553-06-X.


External links
Cyanoptila cyanomelana in Field Guide: Birds of the World on Flickr

The '''blue-and-white flycatcher''' ('''''Cyanoptila cyanomelana''''') is a [[Bird migration|migratory]] [[songbird]] in the Old World flycatcher family [[Muscicapidae]]. The species is also known as the Japanese flycatcher. It breeds in [[Japan]], [[Korea]], and in parts of north eastern [[China]] and the [[Russian Far East]]. It winters in South East Asia, especially in [[Vietnam]], [[Cambodia]], [[Thailand]], [[Sumatra]] and [[Borneo]]. This species has been recorded as a vagrant from the Sinharaja Rainforest in Sri Lanka in 2014.

== Taxonomy and etymology ==
The species was first described in 1829 by [[Coenraad Jacob Temminck]] under the [[binomial name]] ''Muscicapa cyanomelana'', and was transferred to its own genus ''Cyanoptila'' by [[Edward Blyth]] in 1847, on the basis of structural differences from other flycatchers, including its wing length and bill dimensions.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Blyth |first=Edward |title=Notices and Descriptions of various New or Little Known Species of Birds |journal=Journal of the Asiatic Society of Bengal |publisher=Bishop's College Press |volume=16 (1847) |date=1847 |issn=0368-1068 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40130243 |access-date=2026-02-21 |page=124}}</ref>

The genus name comes from [[ancient Greek]]; "''kuanos''" meaning dark blue, and "''ptilon''", plumage. The specific ''cyanomelana'' also comes from "''kuanos''", paired with ''melas'', ''melanos'' meaning dark, black.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Jobling |first=James A. |title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names: From Aalge to Zusii |date=2010 |publisher=Christopher Helm |isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 |edition=1. Aufl. |location=London |page=127}}</ref>

=== Subspecies ===
Two [[subspecies]] are accepted:<ref name="IOC">{{cite web |title=Chats, Old World flycatchers – IOC World Bird List |website=IOC World Bird List – Version 14.2 |date=2025-02-20 |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/chats/ |access-date=2026-02-21}}</ref>

* ''C. c. cyanomelana'' <small>(Temminck, 1829)</small> - breeds in [[Japan]] and the southern [[Kuril Islands]]; it [[bird migration|migrates]] south to winter in [[Myanmar]], [[Thailand]], and the [[Greater Sundas]].
* ''C. c. intermedia'' <small>([[Weigold]], 1922)</small> - breeds in [[northeastern China]] ([[Heilongjiang]] southward to northeastern [[Hebei]], north and east of [[Beijing]]), southeastern [[Russia]] ([[Amur Oblast]] and [[Primorsky Krai]]), and the [[Korea|Korean peninsula]]; it migrates south to winter in Myanmar, Thailand, and the Greater Sundas.

A former third subspecies from further southwest in China is now treated as a separate species, [[Zappey's flycatcher]] ''C. cumatilis''.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Leader |first1=Paul J. |last2=Carey |first2=Geoff J. |date=2012 |title=Zappey's Flycatcher ''Cyanoptila cumatilis'', a forgotten Chinese breeding endemic |url=https://biostor.org/reference/251077 |journal=Forktail |volume=28 |pages=121–128}}</ref>

== Description ==
The blue-and-white flycatcher is a large flycatcher (16–17 cm long, weight 25 g on average) with a striking plumage pattern. The male is deep cobalt blue, with black on the face down to the upper part of the breast and flanks, the rest of the underside white. There is also a prominent white patch on the base of the outer tail feathers. Immature males have the adult male's blue wings, but are otherwise brown like females.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Myers |first1=Susan |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=iLynCwAAQBAJ |title=Birds of Borneo: Sabah, Sarawak, Brunei and Kalimantan |last2=Allen |first2=Richard |date=2016 |publisher=Christopher Helm |isbn=978-1-4729-2445-2 |edition=2nd |series=Helm field guides |location=London New York, NY |pages=}}</ref>

The female is smaller on average than the male,<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Wang |first1=Bin Liu, Libo |last2=Xue |first2=Dandan |last3=Xu |first3=Peng |last4=An |first4=Yuting |last5=Lu |first5=Changhu |date=2021 |title=The Function of a Migration Corridor for a Passerine: A Case Study Based on Age and Gender of Blue-and-white Flycatcher (Cyanoptila cyanomelana) |journal=Pakistan Journal of Zoology |volume=53 |issue=5 |doi=10.17582/journal.pjz/20190620040649 |issn=0030-9923|doi-access=free }}</ref> with head, face and most of upperparts grey-brown, and pale beige-white on the lower part of the middle of the throat, young males are very similar. The female has a smaller-headed profile than other brown flycatchers in range.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Blue-and-white Flycatcher |url=https://ebird.org/ebird/species/bawfly2 |access-date=2025-02-02 |website=[[eBird]] |language=en}}</ref>

The song is a short, melancholic sounding series of whistles.<ref name=":0" />

== Ecology ==

=== Habitat ===
Blue-and-white flycatchers breed in primary and secondary lowland and [[submontane forest]]s (including [[taiga]], wooded slopes, and [[gullies]]) up to 1200 metres, and also in riverine thickets and plantations. Outside the breeding season, it uses coastal woodland, scrub, parks, and gardens, and in [[Borneo]] winters at elevations up to 1850 metres<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Bishop |first=K. D. |date=2017-08-21 |title=J. A. Eaton, B. van Balen, N. W. Brickle, and F. E. Rheindt, ''Birds of the Indonesian archipelago: Greater Sundas and Wallacea'' |journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology |volume=117 |issue=4 |pages=388–389 |doi=10.1080/01584197.2017.1364149 |bibcode=2017EmuAO.117..388B |issn=0158-4197}}</ref> in forested hills, lower montane forest, plantations, and logged lowland forest.<ref name=":1" />

These flycatchers are most active in the morning.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Jeong |first1=Young-Hun |last2=Choi |first2=Sung-Hwan |last3=Banjade |first3=Maniram |last4=Jin |first4=Seon-Deok |last5=Park |first5=Seon-Mi |last6=Kunwar |first6=Binod |last7=Oh |first7=Hong-Shik |date=2024-02-26 |title=Spatiotemporal Niche Separation among Passeriformes in the Halla Mountain Wetland of Jeju, Republic of Korea: Insights from Camera Trap Data |journal=Animals |volume=14 |issue=5 |page=724 |doi=10.3390/ani14050724 |issn=2076-2615 |pmc=10930397 |pmid=38473109 |doi-access=free}}</ref>

=== Breeding ===
The breeding season extends from late May to early August. The nests, constructed primarily of moss, are typically situated in a variety of sheltered locations, including crevices in cliffs, among tree roots, under overhanging stream banks, in shallow tree hollows, and, rarely, within abandoned buildings. The clutch size ranges from four to six eggs, which are incubated solely by the female for a period of 14 to 15 days.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last1=Clement |first1=Peter |last2=Marks |first2=Jeffrey S. |date=2020 |title=Blue-and-white Flycatcher (Cyanoptila cyanomelana), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/bawfly2/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.bawfly2.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Females spend more time and energy caring for young than males.<ref>Li, D.W., Zhao, W.G., Liu, P., Yu, D. and Chen, H., 2017. A study on the reproductive behavior of the Blue–and–white flycatcher in eastern Heilongjiang Province. Heilongjiang Anim. Sci. Vet. Med., 529: 229–233.</ref> [[Nest parasite]]s include the [[northern hawk-cuckoo]], [[common cuckoo]] and [[lesser cuckoo]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=YANG |first1=Canchao |last2=LIANG |first2=Wei |last3=ANTONOV |first3=Anton |last4=CAI |first4=Yan |last5=STOKKE |first5=Bård G. |last6=FOSSØY |first6=Frode |last7=MOKSNES |first7=Arne |last8=RØSKAFT |first8=Eivin |date=2012-03-01 |title=Diversity of parasitic cuckoos and their hosts in China |journal=Chinese Birds |volume=3 |issue=1 |pages=9–32 |doi=10.5122/cbirds.2012.0004 |bibcode=2012AvRes...3....9Y |issn=1674-7674|hdl=11250/2462504 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>

=== Diet ===
It feeds mainly on insects and larvae, but also eats berries,<ref>{{Cite web |title=Blue-and-white Flycatcher |url=https://www.oiseaux-birds.com/card-blue-white-flycatcher.html |access-date=2024-04-21 |website=www.oiseaux-birds.com}}</ref> including [[Phytolacca|pokeweed]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Liu |first1=Bin |last2=Wang |first2=Guohai |last3=An |first3=Yuting |last4=Xue |first4=Dandan |last5=Wang |first5=Libo |last6=Lu |first6=Changhu |date=2021-06-22 |title=Similar seed dispersal systems by local frugivorous birds in native and alien plant species in a coastal seawall forest |journal=PeerJ |volume=9 |article-number=e11672 |doi=10.7717/peerj.11672 |doi-access=free |pmid=34221739 |pmc=8231312 |issn=2167-8359}}</ref>

== Status ==
Although the species population is decreasing, its wide distribution and likely large population size lead to its classification as "[[Least-concern species|Least Concern]]" on the [[IUCN Red List]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=[[BirdLife International]] |title=Blue-and-white Flycatcher ''Cyanoptila cyanomelana'' |url=https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/blue-and-white-flycatcher-cyanoptila-cyanomelana |access-date=2025-02-03 |website=[[BirdLife International]] DataZone |language=en}}</ref>

zh.extract:
白腹藍鶲（香港作白腹姬鶲，台湾作白腹琉璃，学名：Cyanoptila cyanomelana），又稱白腹鶲、白腹藍姬鶲、大琉璃、琉璃鸟、山竹鸟等，香港俗稱蓝燕、藍電。


生态环境
白腹蓝鹟喜欢原始林及次生林的多林地带。


分布地域
白腹藍鶲屬於遷移性鳴鳥，在日本、韓國、中國及俄羅斯繁殖，冬天遷移到東南亞，特別在越南、柬埔寨、泰國、蘇門答臘及婆羅洲等過冬。


特征
雄性的白腹藍鶲與海南藍鶲非常相似，最明顯的分別是胸前藍白色的分界和爪的顏色。白腹藍鶲的胸前藍白交界清澈易見，爪是黑色；而海南藍鶲的藍白交界模糊，爪是肉色。


食物
主要以昆蟲為食。


繁殖与保护
在春天和夏天繁殖期，牠們會發出獨特的“咕—咕”聲。


白腹藍鶲与鸟类贸易


註解
